preamble: The affrication and fortition of the palatal glide [j] into a voiceless postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is a common path in the development of many languages. A notable example is the Latin word 'iam' becoming Italian 'già' [dʒa], with subsequent or parallel developments leading to [tʃ] in other contexts or languages.

examples.note: Initial palatal glide strengthening to an affricate (voiced [dʒ] often parallels voiceless [tʃ]).

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
